ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for garlic black pepper beef rice. FOODON_00002809 (beef dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (beef meat dish) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for garlic black pepper beef rice as a composite Thai dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific composite Thai beef rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of garlic black pepper beef rice is approximately 380 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed jasmine rice and 180 g of stir-fried beef with garlic and black pepper sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Beef stir-fried with garlic and black pepper sauce over steamed rice — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked beef (ID 13397), steamed white rice (ID 20444), garlic (ID 11215), and black pepper (ID 02010), combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Thai stir-fried beef dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for garlic black pepper beef rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked lean beef, steamed jasmine rice, garlic, black pepper, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, weighted by typical recipe proportions (approximately 47% rice, 40% beef, 13% sauce/aromatics by weight). Sodium is elevated due to oyster sauce and soy sauce contributions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled, Thai jasmine) GI ~56–64; composite dish GI estimated at lower bound due to protein and fat co-ingestion
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for garlic black pepper beef rice. GI is estimated at 56 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being Thai jasmine white rice (GI 56–64 per University of Sydney GI Database). The presence of beef protein (~9.2 g/100g) and fat (~3.8 g/100g) attenuates the postprandial glycemic response, supporting use of the lower bound of the jasmine rice GI range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 74.1) / 100 ≈ 41.5, rounded to 41.0. Serving carbohydrate = 19.5 g/100g × 380 g serving = 74.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from oyster sauce and soy sauce (~420 mg/100g; ~1596 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load from soy-based and oyster sauces; if consumed, portion size should be restricted to half a standard serving and paired with low-sodium foods throughout the day to stay within the 2000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~56) driven by jasmine white rice; high glycemic load per full serving (~41)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with full-portion servings due to the high glycemic load from jasmine rice; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion to 100–120 g while retaining the beef component can substantially lower the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (~1596 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~4.9 g per serving) from beef
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the combination of high sodium from condiments and saturated fat from beef warrants portion control; choosing lean cuts of beef, reducing sauce volume, and limiting frequency of consumption to once weekly is advisable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Black Pepper Beef Rice is a Thai-style stir-fried beef dish served over steamed jasmine rice, popular in Singapore's Thai casual dining segment. The dish features tender slices of beef wok-tossed in a bold, aromatic sauce made from fresh garlic, coarsely ground black pepper, oyster sauce, soy sauce, and fish sauce, producing a savory, peppery, and slightly umami-rich flavour profile. The beef is typically stir-fried over high heat to achieve a lightly caramelised exterior while remaining tender inside. The dish is served on a bed of fluffy steamed jasmine rice, which absorbs the sauce and balances the intensity of the black pepper. Nutritionally, a standard 380 g restaurant serving provides approximately 562 kcal, 35 g protein, 14 g fat, and 74 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notably high (~1596 mg per serving) due to the soy-based and oyster sauces used in the stir-fry, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is high per full serving (~41) owing to the jasmine rice base, which is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is dairy-free and provides meaningful amounts of iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and niacin from the beef component. It is commonly found on the menus of Thai casual dining restaurants in Singapore, such as Saap Saap Thai.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for garlic black pepper beef rice was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked lean beef, steamed jasmine rice, garlic, black pepper, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, weighted by typical recipe proportions. GI is analogously estimated from University of Sydney GI Database data for Thai jasmine white rice. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries and are not sourced from a single exact dish entry. Sodium is estimated at 420 mg/100g based on typical oyster sauce and soy sauce contributions in Thai stir-fry recipes; actual restaurant sodium may vary significantly. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on Thai jasmine white rice (GI 56, University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found. The food_id GD001546 is a field survey data seed record from Saap Saap Thai - Funan. The cuisine_tags 'Pad Thai' and 'Thai Noodle' from the source record are not applicable to this rice dish and have been disregarded. Halal status is not confirmed as oyster sauce and soy sauce brands used by the restaurant are unknown. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and carry moderate uncertainty.
